text: no closer in his understanding of it . He walked after her, his boots far louder on the floorboards than her feet, and caught up as she reached the bottom step . “You do not get to say things like that to me and then walk away,” he said . Lara paused with a hand on the railing and twisted to look at him, her expression hard. “I’m not walking . I’m storming .” As though to prove it, she continued up the stairs, stomping her feet. “See? I said what I meant, and I’m doing it !” Ronin followed, despite a brief risk assessment warning a chance of further damage in the process. Humans were volatile; Lara took the term to a new level. An electric tingle arced across his palm. An impulse, perhaps result of damaged coding somewhere deep inside, to reach out and grab her arm. To force her to stop and talk . That would guarantee a poor ending to the confrontation . The heat in his mind faded; the situation was beyond his ability to comprehend. It was almost amusing. This had to be born of a simple miscommunication. She’d interpreted something he said in an unintended fashion — he’d failed to say what he meant — and it escalated into this . He halted at the top of the stairs as a phrase flitted across his processors. What the fuck ? Though he’d never heard Lara say those words together, they were in her voice, and fit the situation perfectly . For the first time in his remembrance, Ronin laughed. It was short, similar to the sound most humans made after being punched in the gut, but it was a laugh . Lara stood in the doorway to her room, already at the end of the hallway. She stared at Ronin as he approached, her jaw agape . “Don’t you dare fucking laugh at me !” She slammed the door shut. It rattled in its frame, the vibrations running through the nearby walls, and Ronin registered displaced air flow over his bared skin. Lara fumbled on the other side. After a muffled curse, the lock clicked into place . Ronin stood in place for a time, optics focused on the door. There was no diffusing the situation; it had already exploded. He didn’t desire an adversarial relationship with Lara. All he wanted — all he told himself he wanted — was to see her dance . He couldn’t leave things like this . He tapped on the door . “ Go . Away .” “Open the door,” he said, moderating his tone to something gentle . “ No .” She’d been right in thinking that doors — especially the relatively flimsy ones inside the house — weren’t an obstacle to bots. But what would he accomplish by breaking in? He was not her master or her owner, not even her keeper. He’d offered her food, comfort, and security . “I’m sorry I upset you , Lara .” His internal clock tracked every passing microsecond, and soon, time seemed to lose meaning. She didn’t answer. Once, there was a faint rustle of cloth that might have been her moving, but it was gone too quickly to be sure . After five minutes and thirty-three seconds, Ronin turned around and walked to his room. He had equipment to care for; weapons to clean, tools to inspect, clothing to mend . Perhaps some rest would cool Lara . Perhaps he needed to be more mindful of what he said to her . I ’m sorry I upset you , Lara . She stared at the door, speechless, her anger vanished. When she’d slammed it shut, she knew he was going to kick it in and teach her a lesson. Determined not to show her fear, she was ready to fight tooth and nail. To the death . Ronin had asked her to open the door. And apologized to her after she refused . What the fuck ? Never in her twenty-three years had she heard of a bot apologizing to a human . His footsteps retreated down the hall, and his door opened and closed softly. She’d come into this agreement assuming she knew all about bots and their nature. Accepting that, even if Ronin didn’t harm her, she’d be nothing more than a pet to him, a curiosity for his amusement. She could have accepted that if it helped her find Tabitha . But Ronin was different. He was unpredictable, surprisingly deep, even kind of funny in an infuriating way. No matter how hard she tried, she couldn’t quite figure him out . Remorse tightened her chest. Though he was good at evading her questions, he hadn’t been lying; he didn’t understand what triggered her outburst. How could he? Even if he displayed signs of other emotions, he had nothing to be insecure about. He couldn’t know how insignificant his words had made her feel . Do you really think I have anything in here that a bot can’t get elsewhere ? Before her thoughts took another dark turn, she forced herself to the bed. Her day had started early, and her encounter with Devon as she left town had only set the tone for the rest of it — the rainstorm and loss of the ring, the ups-and-downs with Ronin … Their last fight had left her physically and mentally exhausted . She pulled back the blanket and tested the bed’s softness with a hand. Would she even be able to sleep on this cushy thing, in a strange place, knowing she was on the wrong side of the wall ? Glancing to the light switch near the door, she paused, taking her lower lip between her teeth . Too tired to go back and turn it off , she told herself. Guess I’m too tired to call my own bullshit , too . She lay down and pulled the blanket up to her chin.
